title: Iron oxide wash on air-dry clay
subtitle: A mineral-tinted surface wash that reads as a natural earthy finish
excerpt: Red iron oxide mixed with water and brushed over air-dry clay surfaces adds a warm, earthy reddish-brown tone. The wash settles into recessed areas and textures, creating a two-tone effect. No kiln needed; the dried wash is sealed with PVA varnish.
sourceNotes: Iron oxide washes are a standard surface treatment across pottery traditions. Red iron oxide is one of the oldest ceramic colorants. Technique adapted for no-kiln context.

glossaryTerms.definition: A mineral pigment used as a pottery colorant. Red iron oxide gives warm earthy tones; yellow iron oxide gives ochre. Both are non-toxic when used as directed and do not require a kiln to function as surface colour in a sealed no-kiln context.
Fully air-dried. The stage at which the clay is ready for surface treatments.

content.text: An
iron oxide

wash is a mix of iron oxide powder and water brushed over dry clay. In kiln-fired ceramics, the colour changes as it fuses into the clay during firing. In this no-kiln version, the oxide keeps its natural reddish-brown colour and is sealed under PVA varnish. The effect works well over textured surfaces: the wash pools in the recesses and wipes off the high points, giving a two-tone finish.

attrs.content: Wear an N95 mask when mixing dry iron oxide powder. Avoid inhaling the powder. Work in a ventilated space. Iron oxide is not acutely hazardous but fine mineral dust of any kind should not be inhaled. Once wet and sealed with varnish, no further hazard applies.

content.text: When to apply
Apply the wash only to fully
bone-dry
pieces. Wet or leather-hard clay is still shrinking and the wash will not sit evenly. A fully dry piece is light and makes a hollow ring when tapped.
Mixing the wash

Measure one part red iron oxide by volume to six to eight parts water in a small container. Stir until fully dissolved; the wash should run easily off a brush with a slight stain. A thicker wash (one to four ratio) gives a heavier, more opaque result. Test on a scrap piece before applying to the finished work.
Applying the wash
Load the mop brush with wash and apply broadly across the entire surface. Work in one direction and do not overbrush; the wash should cover all surfaces including recesses.
Leave for two to three minutes until the wash begins to lose its wet sheen but has not dried fully.

Dampen the sponge and wring it out until barely wet. Wipe across the high points and flat surfaces using a smooth sweeping motion. The recesses retain more wash than the flat areas, giving the two-tone depth.
Allow to dry fully (30 minutes). Apply a second coat if the colour is too pale.
What it looks like when it is right

The finished surface should show a warm earthy reddish-brown that is darker in any recesses, crevices, or impressed texture, and lighter on the high points. The colour should appear evenly applied, not patchy or blotchy. The overall effect reads as a fired terracotta or ancient earthenware surface.
Sealing

Apply two coats of matt or satin PVA varnish over the dried wash. The varnish fixes the oxide, prevents it from marking surfaces, and protects the clay from humidity. The varnish may slightly darken the colour; test on a scrap before sealing the final piece.
Common mistakes
Applying to wet clay. The wash sits on top of surface moisture and dries unevenly.
Wiping before the wash sets. Wiping immediately removes all colour; waiting the two to three minute window gives the two-tone result.
Using too thin a mix. A pale wash requires too many coats and the first is always patchy.
